text:
The purpose of this research was to investigate the effects of different microwave power levels during the vacuum–microwave drying (VMD) process on drying rates of sour cherries and their quality parameters such as temperature profiles, losses of phenolic compounds, antioxidant activities and color changes. In order to make comparison with VMD process, the samples were dried by using convective drying (CD) at 50, 60 and 70 °C. Freeze-drying (FD) was also used as reference drying method. For maintaining the material below the critical thermal threshold (around 1 kg/kg) without any thermal deteriorations during VMD process, the initial microwave powers of 240, 360 and 480 W were applied which were then reduced to the final powers of 240 or 120 W.

All the quality parameters of extracts obtained from thermally treated CDs were negatively affected as compared to the fresh CDs. Such negative effects included reduction in phenolic content, antiradical activity and colour stability, which all coincided with the loss of anthocyanic pigments. VMD, however, outclassed CD and thereby proved to be a more efficient technique than CD and almost comparable to FD. The optimum VMD parameters were 480 W as starting power and 120 W as ending power. The increased content of derivative quercetin and kaempferol compounds suggest that the drying process of VMD retains the shelf life and nutritional value of its resultant product (sour cherry powder) and can thereby be used for all-year-round consumption of fruits.
